perturbation.original_text: EMV has the right to examine the goods on arrival and has Fifteen (15) business days to notify Manufacturer of any claim for damages on account of the condition, grade or quality of the goods, or non-conformity to the Specifications.
perturbation.changed_text: EMV has the right to examine the goods on arrival and has Five (5) business days to notify Manufacturer of any claim for damages on account of the condition, grade or quality of the goods, or non-conformity to the Specifications.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for claims from 'Fifteen business days' to 'Five business days' may be problematic. This may not be sufficient time to conduct a thorough inspection, especially for complex manufactured products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG), Article 39
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 39(1) of the CISG states that the buyer loses the right to rely on a lack of conformity of the goods if he does not give notice to the seller specifying the nature of the lack of conformity within a reasonable time after he has discovered it or ought to have discovered it. Five business days may not be considered a 'reasonable time' under CISG, especially for intricate manufacturing defects requiring extensive inspection.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time EMV has to notify the manufacturer of any claims, from fifteen to five business days. According to the Law Explanation, Article 39(1) of the CISG requires a 'reasonable time' to discover and report non-conformities. The Perturbation Explanation suggests five days might be insufficient for complex inspections. Since no law snippets were provided, the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation were the only resources available for assessment. Since the Law Explanation suggests the modified time is not 'reasonable' under CISG, this creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement in the event of a material breach of the Agreement provided such breach is not remedied within _sixty_ (_60_) calendar days following delivery of notice of such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement in the event of a material breach of the Agreement provided such breach is not remedied within fifteen (15) calendar days following delivery of notice of such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 'sixty calendar days' to 'fifteen calendar days' could be considered insufficient time to rectify significant contractual violations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of European Contract Law (PECL), Article 1:201
perturbation.law_explanation: While Hong Kong law governs the contract, a reduction to 15 days for remedying a material breach may violate principles of good faith and fair dealing, which generally require a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach. Fifteen days might be deemed inadequate for complex breaches involving manufacturing or supply chain issues, potentially leading to claims of unfair termination. PECL Article 1:201 is a relevant principle on good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-201. General Definitions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-201. General Definitions. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other articles of the Uniform Commercial Code that apply to particular articles or parts thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other articles of the Uniform Commercial Code that apply to particular articles or parts thereof: (1) " Action ", in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, set-off, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) " Aggrieved party " means a party entitled to pursue a remedy . (3) " Agreement ", as distinguished from " contract ", means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1-303. (4) " Bank " means a person engaged in the business of banking and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) " Bearer " means a person in possession of a negotiable instrument, document of title , or certificated security that is payable to bearer or indorsed in blank. (6) " Bill of lading " means a document evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of transporting or forwarding goods. (7) " Branch " includes a separately incorporated foreign branch of a bank . (8) " Burden of establishing " a fact means the burden of persuading the trier of fact that the existence of the fact is more probable than its nonexistence. (9) " Buyer in ordinary course of business " means a person that buys goods in good faith , without knowledge that the sale violates the rights of another person in the goods, and in the ordinary course from a per
perturbation.justification: The change from sixty to fifteen days for remedying a material breach creates a legal contradiction because fifteen days may be insufficient, potentially violating the principle of good faith and fair dealing. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address cure periods, it defines "Agreement" as reflecting the bargain of the parties including course of performance. By drastically reducing the cure period, the changed text may undermine the reasonable expectations of the parties and be viewed as a breach of good faith in Hong Kong where applicable contract law exists, as explained by the LLM and the Perturbation Explanation.
